plainMeaning: Let us beg pardon of the revered head-monk (Acharya), who has thirty-six fine qualities, and of the revered scripture-teacher (Upadhyaya), who has twenty-five — O fortunate soul, forgive!
recitation: ācāryajīne khamāvīe re, jehnāṁ guṇa chattrīśa — khamo bhavi [khāmaṇā re]. upādhyāyajīne khamāvīe re, jehnāṁ guṇa paccavīśa — khamo bhavi [khāmaṇā re].

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit ācārya ('one who teaches by conduct', from ā-√car, 'to practise') + the Gujarati honorific -jī + dative case-marker -ne
wordByWord.notes: The vernacular honorific -jī clothes the canonical Parameshthin-rank in everyday devotional warmth; -ne marks him as the object appeased.

wordByWord.gloss: let us appease / let us beg forgiveness of
wordByWord.etymology: Causative-hortative of khamāvavuṁ, from Prakrit khamā- / Sanskrit √kṣam ('to forgive, endure'); first-person plural exhortation
wordByWord.notes: Causative: not merely 'we forgive' but 'we cause-to-forgive', i.e. we approach and entreat the other to release resentment.

wordByWord.etymology: Common Indic geet-particle of address and song-emphasis
wordByWord.notes: Carries the lilt of the khamaṇā-geet; addressed to the singer's own soul.

wordByWord.etymology: Relative pronoun; old-Gujarati jeha (= Skt. yad) + genitive-plural -nāṁ
wordByWord.notes: Anusvāra marks agreement with the plural guṇa that follows.
Used here in the enumerated sense fixed for each rank.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati numeral from Prakrit chattīsa / Sanskrit ṣaṭ-triṁśat
wordByWord.notes: The Acharya's standard reckoning of 36 — commonly 5 sense-restraints + 9 celibacy-guards + freedom from the 4 passions + 5 great vows + 5 conducts + 5 carefulnesses + 3 restraints.
The refrain's command — distinct from the hortative khamāvīe above: the soul is now ordered to grant pardon.

wordByWord.etymology: bhavi = bhavya, 'a soul fit for liberation'; the symbol ૦ (eka-ḍo) marks the omitted continuation of the refrain
wordByWord.notes: The circle ૦ is a scribal 'etc.' sign: the chanter completes the full refrain 'khamo bhavi khāmaṇā re'.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit upādhyāya ('one near whom one studies', upa-adhi-√i) + honorific -jī + dative -ne
wordByWord.notes: The monk charged with teaching the Agamas to the order; paired here with the Acharya as the two living preceptor-ranks.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati numeral from Prakrit pañcavīsa / Sanskrit pañca-viṁśati
wordByWord.notes: The Upadhyaya's standard reckoning of 25 — the 11 Aṅgas + 12 Upāṅgas he masters and teaches, plus caraṇa-sittari and karaṇa-sittari (conduct- and practice-seventies).

literalTranslation: Let us beg forgiveness of the revered Acharya, O!, whose qualities are thirty-six — forgive, O fortunate soul! Let us beg forgiveness of the revered Upadhyaya, O!, whose qualities are twenty-five — forgive, O fortunate soul!
idiomaticTranslation: Come, let us appease the revered Acharya, adorned with his six-and-thirty virtues — forgive, O fortunate soul! And let us appease the revered Upadhyaya, rich in his five-and-twenty — forgive, O fortunate soul!

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having appeased the two perfected ranks — the Arihant (12) and the Siddha (8) — in the previous verse, the song now steps down to the two living guides who actually stand before the disciple: the Acharya, head of the order, and the Upadhyaya, its teacher of scripture. The shift is deliberate and tender. One can offend a liberated Siddha only abstractly; but one offends one's own Acharya and Upadhyaya daily and concretely — by lapses of vinaya (deference), by inattention in study, by disobedience. So the khamaṇā grows more pointed exactly as it nears home. The verse names each by his guṇa-count, the litany of recognition the whole song is built on: thirty-six for the Acharya, the fullest disciplinary profile in the order (sense-restraint, celibacy-guards, freedom from the passions, the great vows, conduct, carefulness and restraint), and twenty-five for the Upadhyaya, reckoned chiefly from the eleven Aṅgas and twelve Upāṅgas he has mastered and transmits. Read together the pair embodies the two wings of the path: the Acharya as the living measure of cāritra (conduct) and the Upadhyaya as the living measure of jñāna (scriptural knowledge) — so to ask their pardon is to bow before both right-action and right-knowledge at once. Notice too the grammatical hinge: khamāvīe is a causative exhortation ('let us move them to forgive'), while the refrain's khamo is a bare imperative addressed inward to one's own bhavya soul — the singer simultaneously entreats the teacher outwardly and commands the heart inwardly to let resentment go.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jain principle that kṣamāpanā is impossible without prior recognition of worth: you cannot sincerely seek pardon from one whose virtue you have not acknowledged, so each appeasement is prefaced by counting guṇas. By pairing the conduct-exemplar (Acharya, 36) with the knowledge-exemplar (Upadhyaya, 25) the verse quietly affirms the jñāna-kriyā-bhyāṁ mokṣaḥ axiom — liberation through knowledge-and-action together. The two counts also carry the song forward arithmetically: added to the previous verse's 12+8, they advance the tally of the Pañca-Parameṣṭhin's hundred-and-eight gunas that the next verse completes with the Sadhu's 27.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: At this point in the fortnightly khamaṇā the reciter brings to mind specific failures of respect and attentiveness toward his own guru and study-teacher — sharp words, skipped duties, half-hearted listening — and releases any grudge he himself holds against them. Dwelling for a breath on each guṇa-count turns a rote line into an examination of conscience: do I honour in my teachers the very virtues I have slighted?

crossReferences.text: Namo Āyariyāṇaṁ; Namo Uvajjhāyāṇaṁ — the third and fourth salutations of the Navkar (Pañca-Parameṣṭhin)
crossReferences.source: Navkāra Mahāmantra, as printed in Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha ed.)

sources.title: Guidelines of Jainism — Navpad (Bhadrabahu Vijay)
sources.consultedFor: Standard guna-counts of the Parameshthins: Acharya 36 and Upadhyaya 25 attributes, and their respective roles (head-preceptor vs scripture-teacher)

sources.title: Wisdom Library — 'Upādhyāya' (definitions across Jainism, Sanskrit, Prakrit)
sources.consultedFor: Etymology and sense of upādhyāya (upa-adhi-√i, 'one near whom one studies') and its Jain role

sources.title: Wisdom Library — 'Ācārya' (definitions across Jainism, Sanskrit, Prakrit)
sources.consultedFor: Etymology and sense of ācārya ('one who teaches by his own conduct', ā-√car) and its Jain role as order-head

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Roots √kṣam ('to forgive/endure') behind khamāvīe/khamo, and guṇa, pañca-viṁśati, ṣaṭ-triṁśat

notes: Verse text preserved verbatim from the source book, including the abbreviation glyph '૦' in 'ખમો ભવિ૦', which is a scribal continuation-mark for the refrain 'khamo bhavi khāmaṇā re' established in verse 1; it is expanded in brackets, not normalized. The exact internal breakdown of the 36 Acharya-gunas and 25 Upadhyaya-gunas varies slightly across Shvetambara handbooks; the totals (36 / 25) are fixed and uncontested, and the breakdowns given are the most widely attested reckonings (Acharya: 5 indriya-restraints + 9 brahmacarya-guptis + 4 kaṣāya-freedoms + 5 mahāvratas + 5 ācāras + 5 samitis + 3 guptis; Upadhyaya: 11 Aṅgas + 12 Upāṅgas + caraṇa-sittari + karaṇa-sittari). 'jehnāṁ' is read as the possessive relative 'whose'. The hortative khamāvīe vs imperative khamo distinction is grammatical and secure.
